Blood and tissue specimens were collected from male Fisher CDF rats administered 25 µg/kg/day SJG-136 via jugular vein catheter for 5 days according to the schedule in the following table.
Day 1 | A total 27 rats will be used. Among them, 21 rats will be dosed at 25 µg/kg/day, dx5, iv.; and the rest will be used as a controls. Blood samples will be collected from 3 rats 30 min after dosing for determining SJG-136. Those 3 rats will be sacrificed 4 h later, and bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC are collected and stored at –80°C for toxicity‑genomic analyses. A portion of PBMC will be examined using comet assay at the Mayo.
Day 2 | 24 h after the 1st dose: 3 rats shall be sacrificed to collect bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC. 4 h after the 2nd dose: 3 rats shall be sacrificed to collect bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC. A portion of PBMC was stored to measure DNA damage using the comet assay.
Day 3 | 4 h after the 3rd dose: 3 rats shall be sacrificed to collect bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC. A portion of PBMC was stored to measure DNA damage using the comet assay.
Day 4 | 4 h after the 4th dose: 3 rats shall be sacrificed to collect bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC. A portion of PBMC was stored to measure DNA damage using the comet assay.
Day 5 | Blood samples shall be collected from 3 rats 30 min after dosing for determining SJG-136. Those 3 rats shall be sacrificed 4 h later to collect bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC. A portion of PBMC was stored to measure DNA damage using the comet assay.
Day 8 | After a 3‑day drug‑free recovery period, the remaining 3 rats shall be sacrificed, and bone marrow, heart, jejunum and PBMC shall be collected and stored at –80°C for toxic‑genomic analyses. A portion of PBMC was stored to measure DNA damage using the comet assay.

The purpose of Phase I of this study was to determine the bioavailability of a single dose of $^{14}$C-ellipticine (NSC-71795) in female beagle dogs and Sprague Dawley rats.
Six healthy female beagles (8-12 months old) were utilized in the Phase I study. Two animals received a single i.v. dose of $^{14}$ C-ellipticine via the cephalic vein ( $1.0727 \times 10^8$ dpm, 35.06 mg - dog number 19394; $1.1187 \times 10^8$ dpm, 36.56 mg - dog number 19391). The dose level was then increased to 0.2 mg/kg/day for 5 days a week, every other week, for 3 courses of treatment. Treatment was then resumed at 0.15 mg/kg/day, 5 times a week approximately every third week, for an additional 8 courses of treatment. Four male and four female beagle dogs were started at a dose level of 0.05 mg/kg, given intravenously once a week. After 4 weeks the dose level was increased to 0.075 mg/kg, once a week, for an additional 14 weeks, at which time the dose was raised to 0.085 mg/kg, once a week. The dose was then increased to 0.1 mg/kg once a week. Since that time the dogs have received 0.1 mg/kg every other week. Six beagle dogs, 3 males and 3 females, were started on 6 mg/kg, given intravenously once a week for four weeks. The dose was decreased to 5 mg/kg for the next 26 weeks. The remaining dogs have continued to receive 5 mg/kg once a week. Two male and two female rhesus monkeys were started at a dose level of 10 mg/kg, once a week. After 11 weeks the dose level was increased first to 12 mg/kg, once a week for 10 weeks, and then to 15 mg/kg, once a week for an additional ten weeks. The dose level has now been increased to 18 mg/kg, once a week. Four male and four female rhesus monkeys were started at a dose level of 3 mg/kg, twice a week. Treatment was then resumed at a level of 1.5 mg/kg, twice a week for the next 3 weeks. The dose level was then reduced to 1 mg/kg, twice a week. After 3 weeks of no treatment, when all animals appeared normal and the WBCs were within normal limits, treatment was started again on these monkeys at a dose level of 1 mg/kg, twice a week. This dosage schedule has been continued for most of the monkeys. One rhesus monkey received 5 mg/kg, given intravenously 3 times a week, for 1 week. A second monkey received 3 mg/kg, 3 times a week, for one week and showed a marked decrease in WBC one week after the last injection. The monkey is now scheduled to receive 5 mg/kg once a week as a maintenance dose.

The rats were intubated intragastrically with water, 5 ml/100 g, 30 to 60 minutes before and again 60 to 90 minutes after intraperitoneal injections of cyclophosphamide (or of 0.85% NaCl). One half hour later they were anesthetized with pentobarbitol sodium, 30 mg/kg given intravenously; their bladders were catheterized, drained, and rinsed with isotonic saline and a continuous intravenous infusion of 5% dextrose was begun at 3 to 4 ml/min. Dose schedule played an important role in the toxicity of L-Alanosine. Cumulative toxicity was suggested by the fact that a total dose of 1250 mg/m² was lethal when spread over 5 or 10 consecutive days while 8,000 mg/m² was necessary to produce lethality when given as a single dose. However, when L-Alanosine was given as six weekly injections a cumulative dose of 12,000 mg/m² was tolerated without lethality indicating increased tolerance when the treatments are followed by periods of rest. Some increased tolerance was also observed when treatments were given by three courses of five daily injections, each course followed by nine days of no treatment. The study will consist of as many trials as necessary to produce a reversal of response plus five trials past the first reversal. The first trial will consist of one mouse receiving a single intravenous dose of 25 mg/kg of the compound dissolved in 50% Hydroxypropyl-β-Cyclodextrin (HPCD; 2.5 mg/mL) at a dosing volume of 10 mL/kg. The mouse will be observed for 24 hours from time of dosing. At the end of the 24 hours, if the mouse is alive, the second trial will consist of another mouse treated at a dose 1.15 times the original dose (the dosing volume can be increased). If the first mouse is dead at 24 hours, the second mouse will be treated at a dose that is equal to the previous dose divided by 1.15. The first mouse will be observed for another 24 hour period wherein, if it dies, the next mouse to be dosed (i.e., the third mouse) will receive a dose that is equal to the dose given to the first mouse divided by 1.15. This pattern will continue for a total of five trials past the first reversal where the dose of the next mouse depends upon the outcome of the previous trial after an observation period of 24 hours. The dose will be divided by 1.15 if the mouse in the previous trial is dead and will be multiplied by 1.15 if the mouse in the previous trial is alive. However, if during the second 24 hour observation period, a mouse dies, the next mouse to be treated will receive a dose that is equal to the dose received by the dead mouse divided by 1.15. Mice in all trials that live to 48 hours will be observed until day 14.
